title: Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India
summary: Held that the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 cannot be restricted except through a procedure that is fair, just and reasonable, fundamentally expanding the scope of fundamental rights in India.
details: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded by the government without furnishing reasons. The Supreme Court examined whether the procedure for deprivation of personal liberty under Article 21 was merely any enacted law or required a just and fair procedure. The Court overruled its earlier decision in A.K. Gopalan and held that Article 21 is not a standalone provision but must be read along with Articles 14 and 19. The Court established that any law depriving a person of personal liberty must satisfy the test of being fair, just and reasonable. This judgment transformed Article 21 into the cornerstone of Indian constitutional jurisprudence.
key_points: Article 21 must be read with Articles 14 and 19 collectively
Procedure established by law must be fair, just and reasonable
Overruled the narrow interpretation from A.K. Gopalan case
Expanded the scope of personal liberty beyond mere physical restraint

title: Olga Tellis vs Bombay Municipal Corporation
summary: Established that the right to livelihood is an integral part of the right to life under Article 21, and that eviction of pavement dwellers must follow principles of natural justice.
details: Pavement and slum dwellers in Bombay faced eviction without rehabilitation. The Supreme Court examined whether the right to livelihood is encompassed within Article 21. The Court held that the right to life includes the right to livelihood, as no person can live without means of subsistence. It ruled that eviction of pavement dwellers was permissible but only if they were given alternative sites or notice and opportunity of hearing. The judgment balanced the state's power to remove encroachments with the constitutional duty to protect life and livelihood of the urban poor.
key_points: Right to livelihood is part of right to life under Article 21
Eviction without rehabilitation violates fundamental rights
Natural justice requires notice and hearing before eviction
State must provide alternative accommodation where possible

title: Vishaka vs State of Rajasthan
summary: Laid down comprehensive guidelines (Vishaka Guidelines) for preventing sexual harassment of women at the workplace, enforceable as law until legislation was enacted.
details: A social worker named Bhanwari Devi was gang-raped for preventing a child marriage in Rajasthan. The Supreme Court examined sexual harassment of women at the workplace as a violation of fundamental rights. In the absence of specific legislation, the Court drew from international conventions including CEDAW to frame guidelines. The Vishaka Guidelines defined sexual harassment, imposed a duty on employers to prevent it, and established complaint mechanisms. These guidelines remained the law until the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 was enacted.
key_points: Sexual harassment at workplace violates Articles 14, 19 and 21
International conventions can be used to fill legislative gaps
Employers must ensure safe working environments
Led to POSH Act 2013 through the guidelines

title: Indira Sawhney vs Union of India
summary: Upheld 27% reservation for Other Backward Classes in public employment and introduced the creamy layer exclusion, while holding that reservation cannot exceed 50% in normal circumstances.
details: The Supreme Court examined the scope of Articles 15(4) and 16(4) concerning reservation for backward classes. The government had implemented the Mandal Commission recommendations for 27% OBC reservation in central services. The Court upheld the reservation but introduced the creamy layer concept, excluding affluent members of OBCs from reservation benefits. It held that reservation is not an exception but a facet of equality, and that the total reserved quota should generally not exceed 50%. The judgment also clarified that reservation in promotions is not constitutionally mandated and that the creamy layer among SC/ST is also subject to exclusion.
key_points: Upheld 27% OBC reservation in public employment
Introduced creamy layer exclusion among OBCs
Reservation generally cannot exceed 50%
Reservation is a tool for substantive equality, not an exception to equality

title: Navtej Singh Johar vs Union of India
summary: Decriminalized consensual same-sex relations between adults by reading down Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, affirming LGBTQ+ dignity and privacy rights.
details: Section 377 IPC criminalized carnal intercourse against the order of nature, used historically to target LGBTQ+ individuals. The Supreme Court examined whether the provision violated fundamental rights to equality, privacy, and freedom of expression. The Court held that criminalizing consensual same-sex relations violates Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21. It declared that sexual orientation is an innate characteristic and that consensual private adult conduct is protected under the right to privacy. Section 377 was read down to apply only to non-consensual acts and bestiality, while consensual same-sex relations were decriminalized.
key_points: Sexual orientation is a protected characteristic under Article 15
Consensual adult same-sex relations are protected under Article 21
Section 377 read down, not struck down entirely
Affirmed dignity and privacy of LGBTQ+ individuals

title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy vs Union of India
summary: Established the right to privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21, holding that it is an intrinsic part of life and personal liberty.
details: The constitutional validity of the Aadhaar biometric identification program raised questions about privacy. A nine-judge bench examined whether the right to privacy is a fundamental right. The Court unanimously overruled previous judgments in M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) that denied privacy as a fundamental right. It held that privacy is an intrinsic and essential part of Article 21 and also protected under Article 19. The judgment identified three facets of privacy: bodily integrity, informational privacy, and decisional autonomy. It established that any state intrusion into privacy must meet the tests of legality, legitimate aim, and proportionality.
key_points: Right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21
Privacy includes bodily, informational, and decisional autonomy
Overruled M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh on privacy
State restrictions on privacy must pass proportionality test

title: Shayara Bano vs Union of India
summary: Declared the practice of instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) as unconstitutional and violative of Muslim women's rights under Article 14.
details: Shayara Bano challenged the practice of instant triple talaq, which allowed a Muslim man to divorce his wife by uttering talaq three times in one sitting. The Supreme Court examined whether this practice was an essential religious practice protected under Article 25. By a 3:2 majority, the Court held that triple talaq is not an essential part of Islam and is manifestly arbitrary, violating Article 14. The Court noted that the practice discriminated against Muslim women and was unconstitutional. This led to the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019, which criminalized instant triple talaq.
key_points: Instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) is unconstitutional
Not an essential religious practice under Article 25
Manifestly arbitrary and violative of Article 14
Led to legislative ban under Muslim Women Protection Act 2019

title: Jeeja Ghosh vs Union of India
summary: Affirmed the right to accessibility and non-discrimination for persons with disabilities, holding that dignity and inclusion are central to the rights framework.
details: Jeeja Ghosh, a person with cerebral palsy, was offloaded from an aircraft due to her disability. The Supreme Court examined whether this treatment violated her rights under the Persons with Disabilities Act, 1995. The Court held that persons with disabilities have the right to equal participation in society and freedom from discrimination. It emphasized that disability does not diminish the right to dignity and that service providers must make reasonable accommodations. The Court awarded compensation and directed airlines to train staff on handling passengers with disabilities. The judgment reinforced the social model of disability over the medical model.
key_points: Persons with disabilities have right to non-discriminatory access
Disability does not diminish the right to dignity
Service providers must make reasonable accommodations
Airlines must train staff on disability sensitivity

title: National Legal Services Authority vs Union of India
summary: Recognized transgender persons as the third gender and declared that fundamental rights under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 apply fully to transgender individuals.
details: NALSA filed a petition seeking legal recognition of transgender persons beyond the male-female binary. The Supreme Court examined the constitutional status of transgender persons and their rights. The Court declared that transgender persons are the third gender and entitled to fundamental rights under Articles 14, 15, 16, 19, and 21. It directed the government to treat transgender persons as socially and educationally backward classes entitled to reservation. The Court also mandated legal recognition of gender identity without sex reassignment surgery and called for affirmative action in education, employment, and healthcare.
key_points: Transgender persons recognized as third gender
Fundamental rights apply fully to transgender individuals
Right to self-identification of gender without medical intervention
Entitled to reservation as backward class

title: S.R. Bommai vs Union of India
summary: Held that secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution and that the President's power to dismiss state governments under Article 356 is subject to judicial review.
details: The dismissal of several state governments under Article 356 following political changes at the Centre was challenged. A nine-judge bench examined the scope of Article 356 and the place of secularism in the constitutional framework. The Court held that secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution and cannot be amended or destroyed. It ruled that the President's satisfaction under Article 356 is not immune from judicial review. The judgment also affirmed federalism as a basic feature and limited the power of the central government to dismiss state governments arbitrarily. This case remains the most authoritative pronouncement on secularism and federalism in India.
key_points: Secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution
Article 356 dismissal is subject to judicial review
Federalism is a basic feature of the Constitution
State governments cannot be dismissed arbitrarily

title: Common Cause vs Union of India
summary: Recognized the right to die with dignity as a fundamental right under Article 21 and legalized passive euthanasia and living wills under strict guidelines.
details: Common Cause, a non-profit, sought legal recognition of living wills and the right to passive euthanasia for terminally ill patients. The Supreme Court examined whether the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to die with dignity. The Court held that the right to life includes the right to a dignified life up to its natural end, and therefore includes the right to refuse medical treatment through a living will. It distinguished active euthanasia (unlawful) from passive euthanasia (lawful under guidelines). The Court laid down detailed procedures for executing living wills and for seeking passive euthanasia in the absence of a living will, with oversight by a medical board and judicial magistrate.
key_points: Right to die with dignity is part of right to life under Article 21
Passive euthanasia legalized under strict guidelines
Living wills recognized as legally valid advance directives
Active euthanasia remains unlawful

title: Suchita Srivastava vs Chandigarh Administration
summary: Held that women with mental disabilities have the right to reproductive autonomy and that forced termination of pregnancy violates their fundamental rights.
details: A woman with intellectual disabilities living in a government shelter became pregnant after alleged sexual assault. The Chandigarh Administration sought to terminate the pregnancy. The Supreme Court examined whether a woman with mental disabilities can be forced to undergo medical termination of pregnancy. The Court held that reproductive autonomy is part of a woman's right to life and personal liberty under Article 21. It emphasized that disability does not negate the right to bodily integrity and reproductive choice. The Court rejected the administration's petition and directed that the woman's views be ascertained. The judgment recognized that even persons with disabilities retain decisional capacity and must be supported in exercising their choices.
key_points: Reproductive autonomy is protected under Article 21
Women with disabilities cannot be forced into medical procedures
Disability does not negate bodily integrity rights
Supported decision-making must guide medical choices for persons with disabilities

title: Lata Singh vs State of Uttar Pradesh
summary: Affirmed the right to marry on the basis of personal choice, holding that inter-caste and inter-religion marriages are lawful and protected under the Constitution.
details: Lata Singh, a major, married a man from a different caste and faced threats and harassment from her family and local authorities. The Supreme Court examined whether adults have the right to marry freely without caste or religious constraints. The Court held that every adult has the right to marry any person of their choice and that caste-based harassment violates fundamental rights. It directed police and authorities to protect inter-caste couples from harassment and threats. The Court emphasized that personal choice in marriage is protected under Article 21 and is essential to individual dignity and autonomy. The judgment directed all states to ensure that no police station harasses or threatens couples in inter-caste or inter-religion marriages.
key_points: Right to marry is a matter of personal choice under Article 21
Inter-caste and inter-religion marriages are constitutionally protected
Police must protect couples from caste-based harassment
Honour crimes and threats violate fundamental rights

title: People's Union for Civil Liberties vs Union of India
summary: Established that the right to food is a fundamental right under Article 21, leading to ongoing judicial monitoring of food security programmes and the Public Distribution System.
details: PUCL filed a public interest litigation during a severe drought, highlighting starvation deaths despite massive food grain stocks with the government. The Supreme Court examined whether the right to food flows from the right to life under Article 21. The Court held that the right to life includes the right to food, and the state has a positive duty to ensure food security for all. Through a series of interim orders, the Court directed universalization of mid-day meals in schools, strengthening of the Public Distribution System, and implementation of food-based welfare schemes. This case remains one of the longest-running PILs in Indian history, with ongoing monitoring by a designated Commissioner.
key_points: Right to food is a fundamental right under Article 21
State has positive duty to ensure food security
Mid-day meal scheme made universal through court orders
Ongoing judicial monitoring of PDS and nutrition programmes

title: Bandhua Mukti Morcha vs Union of India
summary: Held that bonded labour is a violation of fundamental rights under Articles 21 and 23, and that the state is duty-bound to identify, release, and rehabilitate bonded labourers.
details: Bandhua Mukti Morcha, an organization working against bonded labour, petitioned the court about bonded labourers working in stone quarries in Faridabad. The Supreme Court examined whether bonded labour amounts to forced labour prohibited under Article 23 and a violation of the right to life under Article 21. The Court held that any form of forced labour is unconstitutional and that the state has a duty to identify and release bonded labourers. It directed the government to constitute vigilance committees, conduct surveys, and ensure rehabilitation. The Court adopted a broad interpretation of bonded labour, including any labour where the worker is forced to provide service for inadequate wages or under coercive conditions. The judgment also liberalized locus standi, allowing social action groups to approach the court on behalf of vulnerable persons.
key_points: Bonded labour violates Articles 21 and 23
Forced labour in any form is unconstitutional
State must identify, release, and rehabilitate bonded labourers
Social action groups can approach courts on behalf of victims

title: Samatha vs State of Andhra Pradesh
summary: Held that transfer of tribal land to non-tribals in Scheduled Areas is void under the Fifth Schedule and that the state holds tribal land in trust for tribal communities.
details: The State of Andhra Pradesh granted mining leases in Scheduled Areas to private companies, allegedly affecting tribal land rights. The Supreme Court examined whether the Fifth Schedule to the Constitution and the Andhra Pradesh Scheduled Areas Land Transfer Regulation, 1959 prohibit transfer of tribal land to non-tribals. The Court held that the state is a trustee of tribal land and cannot alienate it to non-tribals except in exceptional circumstances. Mining leases granted to non-tribal companies in Scheduled Areas were declared void as they violated constitutional protections for tribal communities. The Court emphasized that tribal land rights are fundamental to the survival and cultural integrity of Scheduled Tribes and that the state must protect these rights against commercial exploitation.
key_points: Transfer of tribal land to non-tribals in Scheduled Areas is void
State holds tribal land in trust for tribal communities
Mining leases to non-tribals in Scheduled Areas are invalid
Tribal land rights are essential to cultural survival

title: Orissa Mining Corporation vs Ministry of Environment and Forests
summary: Held that the rights of forest-dwelling tribal communities under the Forest Rights Act, 2006 must be fully settled before any mining or development project can proceed on forest land.
details: Orissa Mining Corporation sought clearance for bauxite mining in the Niyamgiri Hills, which are sacred to the Dongria Kondh tribal community. The Supreme Court examined the interplay between the Forest Rights Act, 2006 and the Mines and Minerals Act. The Court held that the FRA is a standalone legislation whose procedures for recognizing community and individual forest rights must be completed before any forest land diversion. It directed gram sabhas to decide whether the proposed mining would affect their religious, cultural, and livelihood rights. The gram sabhas unanimously rejected the mining project, effectively protecting the Niyamgiri Hills. The judgment established the primacy of tribal rights and community consent under the FRA.
key_points: Forest Rights Act procedures must precede any forest land diversion
Gram sabhas have the authority to decide on projects affecting their rights
Tribal religious and cultural rights over sacred sites are protected
Community consent is essential under the FRA framework

title: Hussainara Khatoon vs State of Bihar
summary: Established that the right to speedy trial is a fundamental right under Article 21 and that undertrial prisoners cannot be detained longer than the maximum sentence for their alleged offence.
details: A newspaper report revealed that thousands of undertrial prisoners in Bihar jails, many illiterate and poor, had been in custody longer than the maximum sentence for the crimes they were accused of. The Supreme Court examined whether prolonged undertrial detention violates Article 21. The Court held that the right to speedy trial is an integral part of Article 21 and that the state cannot detain undertrials beyond the maximum period of imprisonment for the offence. It ordered the immediate release of all undertrials who had served more than the maximum sentence. The judgment sparked major reforms in the criminal justice system, including the introduction of Section 436A in the CrPC requiring release of undertrials who have served half the maximum sentence.
key_points: Right to speedy trial is fundamental under Article 21
Undertrials cannot be detained beyond maximum sentence
Thousands of undertrials were released following this judgment
Led to legislative reform through CrPC Section 436A

title: Sheela Barse vs Union of India
summary: Addressed the rights of children in custodial institutions, mandating legal aid, human conditions, and judicial oversight of juvenile homes across the country.
details: Sheela Barse, a journalist and social worker, petitioned the court about the deplorable conditions in children's homes and custodial institutions across India. The Supreme Court examined whether children in custody are entitled to legal aid and humane treatment under Articles 21 and 39A. The Court issued comprehensive directions including the right to free legal aid for children, prohibition of handcuffing of children, and regular judicial inspection of juvenile homes. It directed all states to produce information about children in jails and observation homes. The judgment emphasized that children in conflict with law are entitled to protection, care, and rehabilitation rather than punishment, laying the groundwork for the Juvenile Justice framework.
key_points: Children in custody are entitled to free legal aid
Handcuffing of children is prohibited
Juvenile homes must be regularly inspected by magistrates
Children in conflict with law need care, not punishment

title: Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala
summary: Established the basic structure doctrine, holding that Parliament cannot amend the basic features of the Constitution, including judicial review, secularism, federalism, and fundamental rights.
details: The Kesavananda Bharati case is the most significant constitutional law judgment in Indian history. A 13-judge bench examined whether Parliament's amending power under Article 368 was unlimited. The Court held by a 7:6 majority that while Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution, it cannot alter or destroy its basic structure or essential features. The basic structure includes supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secular character, federal character, separation of powers, and fundamental rights. This judgment overruled the earlier decision in Golaknath (1967) which held that fundamental rights are unamendable, and the Court's subsequent decisions in Indira Gandhi election case (1975) and Minerva Mills (1980) further elaborated the doctrine. The basic structure doctrine remains India's unique contribution to global constitutional jurisprudence and is a cornerstone for judicial review of constitutional amendments.
key_points: Parliament cannot alter the basic structure of the Constitution
13-judge bench — the largest bench in Supreme Court history
Basic structure includes secularism, federalism, democracy, fundamental rights
Doctrine unique to Indian constitutional law

title: Lalita Kumari vs Government of Uttar Pradesh
summary: Held that the police must register a First Information Report (FIR) upon receiving information about a cognisable offence — registration of FIR is mandatory, not discretionary.
details: Lalita Kumari, a minor girl, was kidnapped and her father's complaint was not registered as an FIR by the police. The Supreme Court examined whether the registration of FIR is mandatory under Section 154 of the CrPC whenever information discloses a cognisable offence. A five-judge bench held that registration of FIR is mandatory in all cases where information discloses a cognisable offence. The police have no discretion to conduct a preliminary inquiry before registering an FIR for cognisable offences. However, the Court permitted a preliminary inquiry in cases involving matrimonial/family disputes, medical negligence, corruption, commercial offences, and land disputes, but only to ascertain whether the information discloses a cognisable offence. The inquiry must be completed within seven days. This judgment is critical for ensuring access to criminal justice, particularly for women and marginalised communities who often face police apathy in registering complaints.
key_points: FIR registration is mandatory for cognisable offences
No preliminary inquiry allowed before FIR for cognisable offences
Limited preliminary inquiry permitted (7 days) for specific categories
Landmark for access to criminal justice and police accountability

title: M.C. Mehta vs Union of India (Smoking in Public Places)
aka: 2001 AIR SC 1948, Right to Health Case
summary: Held that the right to health is a fundamental right under Article 21 and banned smoking in public places, leading to comprehensive tobacco control legislation in India.
details: Environmental activist M.C. Mehta filed a PIL seeking a ban on smoking in public places, arguing that passive smoking violates the right to life of non-smokers. The Supreme Court examined whether the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to a healthy environment free from tobacco smoke. The Court held that the right to health is an integral part of Article 21 and that smoking in public places violates the rights of non-smokers. It directed the central government to prohibit smoking in public places including hospitals, schools, public transport, auditoriums, and restaurants. The Court also directed the display of health warnings at all cigarette advertisements. This judgment led to the enactment of the Cigarettes and Other Tobacco Products Act (COTPA), 2003, which comprehensively regulates tobacco use in India. The judgment is a landmark for public health law and the right to health jurisprudence.
key_points: Right to health is part of Article 21
Passive smoking violates the rights of non-smokers
Ban on smoking in public places including hospitals, schools, transport
Led to COTPA 2003 comprehensive tobacco legislation

title: Subhash Kumar vs State of Bihar
summary: Established that the right to a pollution-free environment is a fundamental right under Article 21, enabling citizens to approach courts for environmental protection.
details: Subhash Kumar filed a PIL against pollution of the Bokaro River by sludge from a steel plant, arguing that pollution of water sources violates the right to life. The Supreme Court held that the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to a wholesome environment free from pollution. The Court stated that the enjoyment of a pollution-free environment is included in the right to life under Article 21 and that any disturbance of the basic environment elements such as air, water, and soil that is hazardous to life violates the fundamental right. The judgment established that any citizen can file a PIL under Article 32 for environmental protection. This case pioneered the environmental jurisprudence under Article 21 and paved the way for subsequent environmental PILs including the Ganga pollution case and the Vehicular pollution case in Delhi.
key_points: Right to pollution-free environment is part of Article 21
Any citizen can approach the court for environmental protection
Pioneered Article 21 environmental jurisprudence
Pollution of air, water, or soil hazardous to life violates fundamental rights

title: Bachpan Bachao Andolan vs Union of India
summary: Addressed child trafficking and bonded labour, holding that the right to education under Article 21A and the right to protection from trafficking under Article 23 impose positive obligations on the state to rescue and rehabilitate child labourers.
details: Bachpan Bachao Andolan, an NGO led by Nobel laureate Kailash Satyarthi, petitioned the Supreme Court regarding the plight of thousands of children working in circuses, many of whom were trafficked and forced into labour. The Court examined the intersection of child labour, trafficking, and the right to education. It held that children working in circuses are entitled to protection under the Juvenile Justice Act, the Child Labour Act, and constitutional guarantees under Articles 21A, 23, and 24. The Court directed the government to rescue all children working in circuses, provide them rehabilitation, and ensure their education. It also mandated strict enforcement of the Child Labour Act and directed states to conduct inspections of circus establishments. The judgment is a landmark in the fight against child trafficking and for the enforcement of the right to education for rescued child labourers.
key_points: Children in circuses are trafficked and in bonded labour
State must rescue, rehabilitate, and educate rescued children
Articles 21A, 23, and 24 impose positive enforcement obligations
Strict inspection of child labour in entertainment establishments

title: Naz Foundation vs Government of NCT Delhi
summary: Decriminalized consensual same-sex relations among adults by reading down Section 377 IPC, holding that criminalization violates the right to privacy, dignity, and equality under Articles 14, 15, and 21.
details: The Naz Foundation, an NGO working on HIV/AIDS, filed a PIL in the Delhi High Court challenging the constitutional validity of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, which criminalized carnal intercourse against the order of nature. The Delhi High Court held that Section 377 violates the fundamental rights to equality (Article 14), non-discrimination (Article 15), and life and personal liberty (Article 21) insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults in private. The Court recognized that sexual orientation is a protected characteristic and that the right to privacy and dignity extends to all persons regardless of sexual orientation. The judgment was later overturned by the Supreme Court in Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013), but was eventually reaffirmed and expanded by the Supreme Court itself in Navtej Singh Johar (2018). The Naz Foundation judgment was the crucial first step in India's journey toward LGBTQ+ equality and decriminalization of same-sex relations.
key_points: Section 377 IPC violates Articles 14, 15, and 21 for consensual adult acts
Sexual orientation is a protected characteristic
Right to privacy and dignity extends to all persons
Precursor to the Supreme Court's Navtej Johar judgment (2018)
